```text
 Editable working document for keynote development Who Will Ultimately Control the AI Infrastructure that Higher Education Is Coming to Depend On? Deep research dossier and keynote drafting pack Prepared as a full briefing pack: conference reading, abstract stress-test, annotated concept notebook, evidence base, policy agenda, slide storyboard, and Q&amp;A backup. Core framing The conference is asking how higher education should use AI. This keynote asks who will control the infrastructure on which those uses depend. Conference Future Facing Learning and AI in Higher Education 2026, Teesside University Conference dates 15–17 April 2026 Programme slot Day 1 keynote, 1:30–2:15 pm, immediately before the panel on reimagining the purpose and promise of universities Purpose To give you a full set of notes, definitions, evidence, cautions, policy levers, slide architecture, and Q&amp;A backup for drafting the keynote Last updated 17 March 2026 Use this pack as raw material. It is not a finished script; it is a deliberately over-complete set of arguments, evidence, examples, cautions, and speaking lines you can cut down into a 45-minute keynote. Contents • 1. Executive synthesis • 2. Reading the room: conference themes, programme tensions, and audience implications • 3. Stress-testing your abstract • 4. Annotated concept notebook: every note, definition, and linked idea expanded • 5. Evidence base for the keynote • 6. Strategic synthesis: the position your keynote can own • 7. Practical policy agenda for universities • 8. Suggested slide architecture and speaking notes • 9. Backup Q&amp;A • 10. Coverage map of your original notes and selected sources This dossier is designed to be worked with quickly: every main section ends with implications for the talk, and the later sections translate the research into slides, lines, and likely answers. 1. Executive synthesis The conference programme is rich in pedagogy, assessment, ethics, literacy, and practice. Your keynote can do something different and complementary: make the infrastructure question explicit. The strongest version of the talk is not anti-AI and not anti-industry. It is anti-passivity. It argues that universities are moving from occasional tool use to structural dependence, and that once AI is infrastructural the key question shifts from capability to control, accountability, resilience, and bargaining power. The central strategic move is to reframe adoption as governance. Instead of asking only whether a model is impressive, cheap, or convenient, the talk asks who shapes the defaults built into teaching, assessment, advising, analytics, and institutional decision-making. That reframe allows you to connect seemingly separate topics in the programme—assessment redesign, learner analytics, AI literacy, ethics, accessibility, vendor platforms, and institutional strategy—into one coherent argument. The talk works best as a three-step argument. First, AI is already becoming invisible infrastructure in higher education: student use is mainstream, assessment has already changed, and tools are being stitched into everyday workflows. Second, the infrastructure layer is highly concentrated, capital-intensive, and increasingly controlled by external firms whose incentives are not identical to the public mission of universities. Third, lock-in is happening now, while standards, contracts, norms, integrations, and expectations are still fluid. This is the small window in which universities can still move from passive consumption to active stewardship. What the room most needs from this keynote Your keynote should sound like a governance keynote for a room that has mostly been invited to think about pedagogy, productivity, and assessment. That difference is your advantage. It lets you connect the whole programme rather than compete with the sessions already focused on tools. Three claims worth making clearly and early • AI in higher education is no longer just a set of optional tools. It is becoming part of the substrate through which teaching, assessment, support, and governance are delivered. • Infrastructure matters because it shapes what institutions can see, do, audit, export, contest, and change. Control does not have to mean owning every component, but it does require legibility, bargaining power, portability, and evaluation capacity. • The practical choice is not between total in-house sovereignty and total outsourcing. It is between institutions that remain capable stewards of their AI stack and institutions that become captive customers inside an extractive ecosystem. The overall tone to strike • Serious, practical, and public-interest oriented rather than apocalyptic. • Critical of structural dependency, but not dismissive of vendors, open-source communities, or the genuine usefulness of AI systems. • Comfortable with experimentation, but insistent that experimentation without governance becomes lock-in by default. • Focused on educational purpose: what universities are for, what students deserve, and what academic values require from infrastructure. One-sentence takeaway Universities are not only deciding whether to use AI; they are helping fix the defaults that may govern higher education for years, and the question is whether they will do so as passive customers or active stewards. 2. Reading the room: conference themes, programme tensions, and audience implications What the conference is explicitly about. Future Facing Learning and AI in Higher Education 2026 is framed as a multi-day gathering on the opportunities, challenges, and future directions of AI in higher education. The conference pages foreground AI-enhanced pedagogy, research and innovation, responsible AI and digital ethics, and rethinking assessment in the age of AI. The call for contributions broadens this into pedagogy and assessment, learning analytics and data use, equity and inclusion, student voice, institutional strategy, and EdTech practice. What the programme is p
```

```text
ractically about. The scheduled sessions show a room preoccupied with assessment, writing, feedback, student use, practical integration, and institutional adaptation. There are keynotes and sessions on being human in a digital world, designing learning for a world where answers are free, Baymax-or-Terminator style questions about academic writing, learner analytics, oral assessment, AI literacy, prompt design, creative and critical pedagogy, and digital resilience. Why this matters for your keynote. Most sessions ask how universities should use AI. Your keynote can ask who governs the layer on which all of those uses depend. That gives you the 'control plane' perspective for the conference: the structural question beneath the practical ones. Conference-fit matrix Theme in the room What the programme signals What your keynote should add AI-enhanced pedagogy The programme highlights tutoring, feedback, prompt design, learning design, and practical teaching use cases. Show that pedagogic possibilities are shaped by the architecture, data rights, and defaults built into the systems institutions buy or approve. Rethinking assessment Assessment is one of the conference's strongest through-lines, from keynote sessions to sponsor talks and workshops. Argue that assessment redesign is inseparable from infrastructure design: logging, provenance, model access, auditability, and permitted uses are infrastructural questions. Responsible AI and digital ethics The programme includes equity, ethics, inclusion, and resilience concerns. Move the conversation from abstract ethics to operational governance: procurement, data use, evaluation, resilience, and student rights. Institutional change and strategy The call for papers explicitly includes institutional change, strategy, and student voice. Position your keynote as a strategic intervention: universities need decision rights, not just guidance documents. Vendor–institution interface The event includes major sponsor and partner presence across the programme. Critique dependency structurally rather than theatrically. The aim is better partnership terms, not performative hostility. Slot dynamics • Your talk sits on Day 1 immediately after lunch and immediately before a panel on reimagining the purpose and promise of universities. That means you should open concrete and vivid rather than theoretical. A service outage, a procurement question, or a lock-in example will land better than a long conceptual preamble. • Because the keynote hands into a panel about university purpose, you have an ideal closing move: translate your argument into three or four decisions institutions must make about the kind of university they want to be in an AI-mediated environment. • The earlier programme includes keynotes and provocations about assessment and learning design. That gives you permission to move up a level and talk about the conditions under which any of those pedagogic ambitions remain accountable. Sponsor ecology and why it is relevant The sponsor mix is part of the substance of the keynote, not just background. The conference pages present AWS as headline sponsor, with additional visibility for companies such as Kortext, FeedbackFruits, and Plato. Their messaging is useful because it shows what the market is already offering universities: institutionally governed AI, analytics visibility, approved content layers, assessment support, and integrated learning design. In other words, the room is already being invited to think in terms of vendor-mediated AI infrastructure. That is not a problem to denounce. It is a reason to sharpen the talk. The room contains people who buy, integrate, govern, research, critique, and market AI systems. Your argument should therefore be: partnerships are inevitable and often valuable, but universities need the capacity to set terms, evaluate claims, preserve exit options, and align systems with academic values. Useful line for the room This is the right room to say: the question is not whether universities should work with industry. They already do. The question is whether those relationships remain governable, reciprocal, and consistent with the public mission of higher education. Audience map Audience segment What they are likely listening for What to emphasise Senior leaders Risk, mission, reputation, cost, and strategic positioning Use language of resilience, bargaining power, public value, and long-term institutional autonomy. Learning technologists and digital teams Integration, support burden, standards, and operations Talk about interoperability, service continuity, model evaluation, and fallback options. Academic staff Assessment, writing, feedback, judgment, and workload Connect infrastructure choices to pedagogy and professional purpose rather than generic efficiency claims. Researchers and doctoral students Conceptual framing and evidence quality Bring in Lessig, path dependence, extractivism, industry concentration, and grounding debates with care. Vendors and partners Credibility and fairness Acknowledge value creation; focus on transparent claims, data terms, shared standards, and trust-building. Bottom line for conference fit This keynote should function as the programme's infrastructure argument. It should not try to outdo the tool demos, nor out-moralise the ethics sessions, nor out-speculate the futurists. Its job is to connect those strands and show that procurement, governance, evaluation, and sector collaboration determine whether all the other hopes on the programme become public-interest infrastructure or a new layer of dependency. 3. Stress-testing your abstract Your abstract is already strong because it makes a structural claim rather than a generic capability claim. It avoids both hype and refusal, and it names the practical levers—procurement, data governance, evaluation, collaboration—that convert the keynote from diagnosis to action. The best use of this section is to sharpen how each sentence can work in the talk. Abstract sentence What it is doing How to make it work in the keynote “Artificial intelligence is becoming part of the invisible infrastructure of higher education.” This is your conceptual hinge. It says AI has moved from visible, optional tools into the background systems and workflows through which work happens. Open with an outage or workflow dependency so 'infrastructure' feels concrete rather than metaphorical. “It no longer exists as a set of optional tools, but underpins how universities teach, assess, advise and govern.” This expands the scope from classroom tools to institutional operations. It is important because it reaches into assessment, support, analytics, and governance. Use examples across four domains: teaching assistance, assessment redesign, student support, and institutional analytics/decision systems. “Much of this infrastructure is built, owned and maintained by external organisations whose incentives often don’t align with the public mission of universities.” This is the political-economy sentence. It moves from adoption to control, ownership, and incentive alignment. Support this with industry concentration, cloud dependence, and sponsor/ecosystem analysis, but keep the tone measured: misalignment is structural, not personal. “This keynote asks a simple question: who will control the AI infrastructure that higher education is increasingly dependent on?” Excellent framing question. It is memorable, open enough to invite reflection, and practical enough to guide the entire talk. Repeat it at least three times during the keynote: once near the opening, once after the evidence base, and once in the conclusion. “Drawing on trends in AI investment, the fragility of essential digital services, and the effects of surveillance capitalism and data extractivism …” This gives you three evidence pillars: economic concentration, operational fragility, and political-economic critique. Keep each pillar concrete: capex and market share for investm
```

<tool-use data-tool-type="shell" data-tool-name="Bash"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Bash**</summary>
Read more of dossier

`cd "/Users/stugrey/Documents/Student Voice ICD/Conferences+Webinars/Teesside Keynote" && unzip -p keynote_ai_infrastructure_he_dossier.docx word/document.xml 2>/dev/null | python3 -c "import sys, re; t=sys.stdin.read(); t=re.sub(r'<[^>]+>', ' ', t); t=re.sub(r'\s+', ' ', t); print(t[14000:22000])"`

```text
ent; Canvas/Teams/ICC examples for fragility; Zuboff/Couldry–Mejias/Pasquinelli for extractive logics. “Higher education is at a crossroads.” This is the decision point of the talk. It prevents the keynote from becoming descriptive only. Make the crossroads visible on a slide: passive customer in an extractive ecosystem versus steward/co-designer of critical systems. “By focusing on practical policy choices around procurement, data governance, model evaluation and sector-level collaboration …” This rescues the talk from abstraction. It also distinguishes your keynote from broader cultural commentary. This section should be crisp and constructive. Give the audience a minimum move, a better move, and a long-term move for each lever. “Ensuring that future AI infrastructure remains accountable to academic values and student interests.” This is the moral landing point. It names who governance is for. End with students, academic purpose, and public mission rather than with a technical or vendor comparison. What the abstract quietly gets right • It treats AI as infrastructure rather than as a novelty. That is more analytically useful and more future-proof. • It avoids the false choice between naive enthusiasm and total rejection. • It identifies the correct policy levers. Procurement and evaluation are often missing from higher education discussions that stay at the level of pedagogy or ethics. • It foregrounds accountability to academic values and student interests, which stops the talk from drifting into abstract sovereignty rhetoric. What to sharpen in the live talk • Define invisible infrastructure fast and concretely. Do not assume the room will instantly hear the depth of the claim. • Separate the argument into a descriptive half and a normative half: first explain the dependency, then explain what should be done. • Use one or two examples of fragility that are educationally legible before moving to broader geopolitical or theoretical cases. • Name the third position explicitly: neither passive consumerism nor romantic self-sufficiency, but active stewardship. • End with institutional decisions, not just concerns. 4. Annotated concept notebook: every note, definition, and linked idea expanded This section expands every topic in your note list and turns it into usable keynote material. For each item, the aim is the same: define the concept, explain why it matters for this talk, show how to use it, and flag any caveat or overclaim to avoid. How to use this section A good keynote does not need to mention all of these concepts explicitly. The value here is optionality. You can choose two or three main frameworks for the spoken talk and keep the rest as backup material for transitions, questions, or later writing. The Benefits of Bubbles: when speculative booms build durable infrastructure Definition: Ben Thompson's argument is that some bubbles are socially productive because overinvestment can finance infrastructure and ecosystem formation before ordinary market logic would. The bubble may still be a bubble, but it can leave behind assets that become genuinely useful. Deep dive: For your keynote, this is valuable because it stops the analysis from collapsing into 'AI hype is fake'. The stronger claim is that AI may well be in an overinvestment phase and still be building real compute capacity, software stacks, integration layers, and organisational habits that higher education will end up using. The question then becomes not whether the boom is entirely rational, but who owns the infrastructure after the exuberance settles and under what conditions universities gain access to it. How it helps the keynote: Use this idea to frame the AI boom as politically consequential rather than merely speculative. It lets you say: even if the bubble produces socially useful infrastructure, universities should not confuse access with control. The public value question begins after the data centres are built. Caution: Do not imply that all AI spending is wise or that every bubble is productive. Your contrast is between bubbles that leave behind infrastructure and bubbles that leave behind almost nothing of public value. Possible line: The question is not whether the AI boom will build useful infrastructure. It probably will. The question is who gets to govern that infrastructure when universities start to treat it as normal. Bad bubbles: tulips, Beanie Babies, Pogs, Panini stickers, bitcoin as rhetorical contrasts Definition: These examples function as shorthand for speculative manias in which asset prices detach from durable underlying value, or at least from the kind of shared infrastructure value that would justify broad public dependence. Deep dive: They matter because they help you distinguish between two kinds of hype story. One is simple speculative frenzy. The other is an investment surge that can also accelerate real build-out. AI looks more like the second category than the first because it is tied to datacentres, chips, networking, developer tooling, and enterprise integration, even if many valuations and claims may still be inflated. How it helps the keynote: Use these examples briefly and rhetorically, not as the centre of the analysis. They are useful for a sentence or a slide contrast: not every bubble leaves behind railways, fibre, or compute; some only leave behind collectibles and regret. Caution: Do not spend long on financial analogies. The point is simply to create conceptual contrast before moving back to higher education. ICC account disruption and the digital-sovereignty lesson Definition: This note points to the widely discussed 2025 controversy around the International Criminal Court and Microsoft services. Reporting and later parliamentary answers make clear that the exact mechanics were disputed, so the safest framing is not 'Microsoft simply switched off the ICC', but that the episode exposed anxiety about dependence on cloud and software infrastructures that can be affected by sanctions, legal pressure, and geopolitical conflict. Deep dive: The deep importance of the example is that infrastructure risk is not only about outages or price rises. It is also about jurisdiction, coercion, continuity, and the ability of institutions to keep operating when political pressures hit the layers they depend on. Microsoft's later European cloud commitments and resilience promises show that the episode was serious enough to trigger public reassurance and policy debate. How it helps the keynote: Use this example later in the keynote, after simpler educational examples such as a learning-platform outage. It expands the argument from operational fragility to sovereignty and control. It says: dependence on external infrastructure is never only a technical matter. Caution: Be precise. Say that the case raised digital-sovereignty concerns and that details of the account interruption were contested. Avoid oversimplified claims that could be rebutted easily. Lessig's four modalities of regulation: law, norms, market, and code Definition: Lawrence Lessig's key insight is that behaviour in digital environments is regulated by at least four interacting forces: law, social norms, markets, and code or architecture. Code matters because it determines what is possible, easy, difficult, visible, or impossible. Architecture is therefore not neutral background; it is a regulator. Deep dive: This framework is particularly useful for higher-education AI because universities often talk as if policy alone will solve governance. Lessig helps show why policy documents are only one modality. Law may set obligations, but market concentration limits choices, local norms affect what staff and students actually do, and code determines defaults such as data retention, logging, explainability, exportability, and access control. How it helps the keynote: Use Lessig as the bridge between abstract ethics and concrete system design. It lets you say that procurement is governance because
```

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-6 2026-04-14 14:48:30Z)**_

Here are drafts for each of the three questions, based on your dossier and slide notes. Feel free to tweak.

---

**1. How to introduce you**

> Our Day 1 keynote is Dr Stuart Grey. Stuart holds a PhD in distributed AI systems — his doctoral work was on distributed agents for autonomous spacecraft — and he teaches part-time at the University of Glasgow. He's also the founder of Student Voice AI, a spin-out that analyses free-text student feedback for universities across the UK, including eight Russell Group institutions. That combination — hands-on AI research, embedded teaching experience, and a close-up view of how universities are actually using these tools — is what he's bringing to today's talk.

**2. How to introduce the talk**

> Most of this conference asks *how* universities should use AI. Stuart's keynote asks a different question: *who* will control the AI infrastructure that higher education is coming to depend on? His argument is that AI has quietly shifted from a set of optional tools into the invisible infrastructure behind teaching, assessment, advising and governance — and that most of that infrastructure is built and owned by external organisations whose incentives don't always line up with the public mission of universities. The talk is neither anti-AI nor anti-industry; it's about governance, and about the practical choices universities still have while the defaults are being set. Please welcome Dr Stuart Grey.

**3. Suggested Q&A questions**

Pick whichever two land best for you — they're designed to let you restate your key moves rather than send you into new territory:

1. *"You frame this as a choice between being a 'passive customer' and an 'active steward' of AI infrastructure. For a university that knows it can't build its own stack, what does active stewardship actually look like in practice — what's the minimum viable version?"*
2. *"You run Student Voice AI and work directly with Russell Group universities. What are you seeing on the ground right now that makes you think this infrastructure question is urgent rather than something we can revisit in a few years?"*
3. *"If a VC or head of digital in this room goes back to their institution on Friday morning, what's the one procurement or governance decision you'd want them to look at first?"*

Let me know if you'd like any of these reworded, shortened, or pitched differently — e.g., warmer, more provocative, or more leadership-focused.
